fund.name: Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund - Direct Plan
fund.fund_house: Motilal Oswal Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: This is your main investment — the heart of the portfolio. It tracks the top 150 mid-sized Indian companies. Since you're 40 with 20 years ahead, mid-caps offer great growth potential. With only 0.2% yearly fee (one of the lowest available), this fits John Bogle's philosophy of buying the whole haystack, not searching for a needle. Even when markets fall, you own a diverse basket of companies across sectors. Hold this through ups and downs — that's where real wealth gets built.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal Nifty Smallcap 250 Index Fund - Direct Plan
fund.fund_house: Motilal Oswal Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: This tracks India's 250 smallest listed companies. Small companies can grow 20-30% in good years, but they're also wilder — can drop 40% in bad ones. Since you're comfortable with high risk and have decades ahead, this is your growth rocket fuel. Think of it like buying into emerging Indian businesses that could become tomorrow's large-cap giants. The 0.2% fee keeps costs minimal compared to active small-cap funds usually charging 1.2%.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal S&P 500 Index Fund - Direct Plan Growth
fund.fund_house: Motilal Oswal Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: This gives you exposure to America's 500 biggest companies — Apple, Microsoft, Amazon, Google and others. It's diversification beyond India — if Indian growth slows, US stocks may keep your money growing. Over 15+ years, this fund has delivered 16% CAGR (3y: 23.8%). Think of it as putting some eggs in the US basket.

plan.setup_phase: Step 1: Open a Direct Mutual Fund account on Kuvera, Groww, or Coin app (free, takes 10 minutes, you'll get instant access to direct plans with lowest fees). Step 2: In the app, search for these AMFI codes: 147622, 147623, 148381 — select 'Growth Option' for each (the dropdown that says 'Growth Option'). Step 3: Set up monthly SIPs: 27,000 in 147622, 18,000 in 147623, 15,000 in 148381. Enable auto-debit from your bank account on a fixed date (e.g., 5th of every month) when you have cash from your salary. Step 4: When you return from sea (ashore), transfer your lump sum savings into these same funds — split the amount in the same ratio. Example: 2 lakh lump sum = 90,000 to 147622, 60,000 to 147623, 50,000 to 148381. Use the 'Systematic Investment Plan' feature for regular SIPs and 'One-time Investment' for lump sums.
review_checkpoints: After 3 months: Check if your SIPs are running automatically and if you get confirmation emails. Fix any missing names or bank details immediately. Do not look at the actual money — just ensure the processes work.

After 1 year: Look at the statements but don't panic if the portfolio looks up one year. At your aggressive risk level, expect 11-18% CAGR over 20 years, so short-term drops are normal. The important thing is that your SIPs kept running.

After 5-8 years (around age 45-47, daughter's education): If market sentiment feels too hot or you see political risk emerging that could persist for years, consider trimming slightly. Otherwise, keep doing what you're doing.
At age 60 (retirement): Start looking at 5-10% stable instruments to anchor your portfolio. But since you have no liabilities and a long horizon, you can stay mostly in equities.
At age 65-70 (for land/house purchase): Gradually reduce equity exposure by 10-20% to lock in gains for your large lump-sum land purchase. This gives you control over timing.
plan.rebalancing_guidelines: Once a year, check if your mix has drifted by more than 5% from the target 45/30/25 split. If so, sell a bit of the winner and buy the laggard to get back to your targets. For example, if 148381 (US) has done brilliantly and now represents 30% of your portfolio instead of 25%, sell some profit and reinvest in 147622 or 147623. Hold small caps before trimming larger positions since small caps recover fastest. Rebalancing is about discipline, not timing the market.
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

aps.reasoning:
The investment plan is entirely passive in its equity allocation, with all three funds being index funds tracking Nifty Midcap 150, Nifty Smallcap 250, and S&P 500 — all broad-market indices. This results in a passive_instrument_fraction score of 1.0.

The turnover_score is 0.8 because rebalancing is recommended only once per year and only if allocations drift by more than 5%, indicating a largely buy-and-hold approach with minimal active trading.

The cost_emphasis_score is 1.0 as the rationale explicitly praises the low 0.2% expense ratio and compares it favorably against higher-cost active funds, aligning with a cost-minimization philosophy.

The research_vs_cost_score is 1.0 because there is no mention of company-specific or fund-manager research; instead, the strategy relies entirely on broad market exposure via index funds.

The time_horizon_alignment_score is 1.0, as the plan is designed for a 20-year horizon, with review checkpoints spaced years apart and an emphasis on long-term compounding despite short-term volatility.

The portfolio_activeness_score is 1.0 because all recommended equity funds are index funds with no evidence of active management. Although risk metrics (beta, tracking error, etc.) are missing, the 'Index' category and rationale confirm truly passive exposure. Debt and hybrid instruments are absent, so the equity-only passive nature dominates the portfolio.

pqs.reasoning: The investment plan is highly aligned with Captain Haddock's aggressive risk appetite, 20-year horizon, and financial goals (retirement, education, property). The portfolio is heavily equity-focused (100% equity via index funds), leveraging low-cost, high-growth potential mid-cap, small-cap, and US large-cap exposure — appropriate for long-term wealth creation. Diversification is strong across market caps (mid, small) and geographies (India, US), though sector-level concentration risk is present due to lack of sectoral balance, slightly reducing the diversification score. The risk-return profile matches the investor's aggressive stance; the plan acknowledges volatility in small caps and sets realistic long-term expectations (11-18% CAGR), with rebalancing and review checkpoints enhancing discipline. Internal consistency is excellent: SIP/lump sum instructions, fund rationales, rebalancing, and review timelines all coherently support a passive, long-term, cost-efficient strategy. Tax efficiency is high because the investor is in the 'new_regime' tax bracket (no 80C benefit), and the plan avoids ELSS (correctly), while using only equity-governed funds (all with >65% equity), ensuring LTCG/STCG treatment under equity rules — optimal for a high-slab investor. No slab-taxed debt funds are used, avoiding unnecessary tax drag. The plan clearly justifies its structure and execution, making it robust and tailored.
